title_en: The Greatest Angels: Jibril, Mikail, Israfil
content_en:
Among the vast host of angels, the Islamic tradition names several by name and describes their specific roles and characteristics. The three most prominent — Jibril, Mikail, and Israfil — represent respectively the dimensions of divine communication, divine provision, and divine transformation through the end of one world-age and the beginning of another.

Jibril (Gabriel) is described in the Quran with the titles Ruh al-Qudus (the Holy Spirit, i.e., Spirit of Holiness), Ruh al-Amin (the Trustworthy Spirit), and the one who descended with the Quran upon the Prophet's heart. He is the greatest of the named angels and the most prominent in Quranic narrative. His primary mission is the delivery of divine revelation to the prophets and messengers, making him the essential link between the divine knowledge and human prophetic guidance. The Quran describes him as 'one of great power, possessing high status with the Lord of the Throne, obeyed there and trustworthy.' He appeared to the Prophet Muhammad in his original form on two occasions, filling the entire horizon — an experience so overwhelming that the Prophet's companions could not comprehend it except through description.

Mikail (Michael) is named in the Quran in Surah Al-Baqarah (2:98) alongside Jibril. The tradition associates Mikail primarily with the management of natural phenomena — rain, vegetation, and provision for living creatures. He is described as overseeing the winds, clouds, and the distribution of sustenance across the earth. The believer who reflects on the regularity of rain, the greening of the earth after drought, or the provision of food and water that sustains all life is, in an Islamic understanding, contemplating the ongoing work of Mikail in administering the material sustenance of creation.

Israfil is not named in the Quran but is mentioned frequently in authentic hadith and is the angel entrusted with the Trumpet (As-Sur). It is he who will blow the Trumpet twice — once to cause the death of all living things at the termination of this world-age, and once to cause the resurrection of all the dead for the Day of Judgment. The Prophet is reported to have said: 'How can I be at ease when the holder of the trumpet has placed it to his lips, drawn in his breath, and is waiting for the command to blow?' This image conveys the constant nearness of the cosmic end and the role of Israfil as the agent of that transformation.

Al-Ashqar also covers other named angels: Israfil's companion, Mika'il; Azrail (the Angel of Death, referred to in the Quran as Malak al-Mawt); the carriers of the divine Throne; the guardian angels; and the scribes Kiraman Katibin who record human deeds. Together, these named angels provide a specific populated picture of the angelic world and its relationship to human life.
content_id:
Di antara sekian banyak malaikat, tradisi Islam menyebutkan beberapa di antaranya dengan nama dan menggambarkan peran serta karakteristik spesifik mereka. Tiga yang paling menonjol, yaitu Jibril, Mikail, dan Israfil, mewakili masing-masing dimensi komunikasi ilahi, pemberian rezeki ilahi, dan transformasi ilahi melalui berakhirnya satu zaman dunia dan dimulainya zaman yang lain.

Jibril digambarkan dalam Al-Quran dengan gelar Ruhul Qudus (Ruh Kesucian), Ruhul Amin (Ruh yang Terpercaya), dan yang turun membawa Al-Quran ke dalam hati Nabi. Beliau adalah yang terbesar di antara malaikat yang disebutkan namanya dan yang paling menonjol dalam narasi Al-Quran. Misi utamanya adalah menyampaikan wahyu ilahi kepada para nabi dan rasul, menjadikannya penghubung esensial antara pengetahuan ilahi dan petunjuk kenabian bagi manusia. Al-Quran menggambarkannya sebagai yang memiliki kekuatan besar, pemilik kedudukan tinggi di sisi Tuhan Pemilik Arsy, yang ditaati di sana dan terpercaya. Beliau menampakkan diri kepada Nabi Muhammad dalam wujud aslinya pada dua kesempatan, memenuhi seluruh cakrawala, suatu pengalaman yang begitu luar biasa sehingga para sahabat Nabi tidak dapat memahaminya kecuali melalui deskripsi.

Mikail disebutkan namanya dalam Al-Quran dalam Surah Al-Baqarah (2:98) bersama Jibril. Tradisi mengaitkan Mikail terutama dengan pengelolaan fenomena alam, yaitu hujan, tumbuh-tumbuhan, dan rezeki bagi makhluk hidup. Beliau digambarkan mengawasi angin, awan, dan distribusi penghidupan di seluruh bumi. Seorang mukmin yang merenungkan keteraturan hujan, menghijaunya bumi setelah kekeringan, atau tersedianya makanan dan air yang menopang seluruh kehidupan sedang, dalam pemahaman Islam, merenungkan karya Mikail yang terus berlangsung dalam mengatur penghidupan material ciptaan.

Israfil tidak disebutkan namanya dalam Al-Quran tetapi sering disebutkan dalam hadis sahih dan merupakan malaikat yang dipercayakan dengan Sangkakala (As-Sur). Dialah yang akan meniup Sangkakala dua kali, sekali untuk menyebabkan kematian semua makhluk hidup pada akhir zaman ini, dan sekali untuk menyebabkan kebangkitan semua orang mati pada Hari Kiamat. Nabi dilaporkan bersabda: Bagaimana aku bisa tenang ketika pemegang sangkakala telah meletakkannya di bibirnya, menarik napas, dan menunggu perintah untuk meniup? Gambaran ini menyampaikan kedekatan abadi dari akhir kosmik dan peran Israfil sebagai agen transformasi tersebut.

Al-Asyqar juga membahas malaikat-malaikat lain yang disebutkan namanya: Azrail (Malaikat Maut, yang disebut dalam Al-Quran sebagai Malakul Maut), para pembawa Arsy ilahi, malaikat-malaikat penjaga, dan para pencatat Kiram Katibin yang mencatat amal manusia. Bersama-sama, malaikat-malaikat yang disebutkan namanya ini memberikan gambaran yang terperinci tentang alam malaikat dan hubungannya dengan kehidupan manusia.
content_ar: Among the vast host of الملائكة, the Islamic التراث names several by name and describes their specific roles and characteristics. The three most prominent — جبريل ﷺ, Mikail, and Israfil — represent respectively the dimensions of divine communication, divine provision, and divine transformation through the end of one الدنيا-age and the beginning of another. جبريل ﷺ (جبريل ﷺ) is described in the القرآن الكريم with the titles Ruh al-Qudus (the Holy الروح, i.e., الروح of Holiness), Ruh al-آمين (the Trustworthy الروح), and the one who descended with the القرآن الكريم upon النبي ﷺ's القلب. He is the greatest of the named الملائكة and the most prominent in القرآني narrative. His الأساسي mission is the delivery of divine الوحي to the الأنبياء and الرسل, making him the الجوهري link between the divine العلم and human prophetic الهداية. The القرآن الكريم describes him as 'one of great power, possessing high status with the Lord of the Throne, obeyed there and trustworthy.' He appeared to النبي ﷺ محمد ﷺ in his original form on two occasions, filling the entire horizon — an experience لذا overwhelming that النبي ﷺ's الصحابة could not comprehend it except through description. Mikail (Michael) is named in the القرآن الكريم in السورة Al-Baqarah (2:98) alongside جبريل ﷺ. The التراث associates Mikail primarily with the management of natural phenomena — rain, vegetation, and provision for living creatures. He is described as overseeing the winds, clouds, and the distribution of sustenance across the earth. The المؤمن who reflects on the regularity of rain, the greening of the earth بعد drought, or the provision of food and water that sustains all الحياة is, in an Islamic الفهم, contemplating the ongoing work of Mikail in administering the material sustenance of creation. Israfil is not named in the القرآن الكريم but is mentioned frequently in authentic الحديث and is the angel entrusted with the Trumpet (As-Sur). It is he who will blow the Trumpet twice — حين to cause the الموت of all living things at the termination of this الدنيا-age, and حين to cause the البعث of all the dead for the يوم القيامة. النبي ﷺ is reported to have said: 'How can I be at اليسر عندما the holder of the trumpet has placed it to his lips, drawn in his breath, and is waiting for the command to blow?' This image conveys the constant nearness of the cosmic end and the role of Israfil as the agent of that transformation. Al-Ashqar أيضاً covers other named الملائكة: Israfil's companion, Mika'il; Azrail (the Angel of الموت, referred to in the القرآن الكريم as Malak al-Mawt); the carriers of the divine Throne; the guardian الملائكة; and the scribes Kiraman Katibin who record human deeds. Together, these named الملائكة provide a specific populated picture of the angelic الدنيا and its relationship to human الحياة.
